correctAnswer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted between forms
acceptableAlternatives: total energy of an isolated system is constant
quizQuestion: What is the fundamental statement of conservation of energy?
explanation: Energy is conserved: it can be converted between forms (KE ↔ PE ↔ thermal ↔ chemical ↔ etc.) but the total never increases or decreases in an isolated system. When friction 'destroys' mechanical energy, it actually converts it to thermal energy — total energy is unchanged. This is one of the most fundamental principles in all of physics.
visualDescription: An energy pie chart that stays the same size while slices (KE, PE, thermal) change proportions
distractors: Energy is created when work is done
Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions
Energy decreases over time in all systems
Potential energy is the fundamental energy form
Energy is conserved only for conservative forces
Mechanical energy equals total energy always
Energy is created by springs and gravity

correctAnswer: W_gravity = mgh (positive when object descends height h)
quizQuestion: What is the work done by gravity on an object that descends a vertical height h?
explanation: W_gravity = mgh (positive for descent). When an object falls, gravity (downward) acts in the same direction as displacement (downward), so W = Fd cos 0° = mgΔh = mgh > 0. When rising, gravity does negative work: W = −mgh. This is why gravitational PE = mgh: the PE decreases (ΔU = −W_gravity) as the object descends.
visualDescription: A ball falling height h with gravity arrow and displacement both pointing down, showing positive work W = mgh
distractors: W_gravity = mgh only when falling vertically

correctAnswer: The object's kinetic energy increases (it speeds up)
quizQuestion: According to the work-energy theorem, what happens to an object's motion when positive net work is done on it?
explanation: W_net = ΔK. Positive net work → ΔK > 0 → KE increases → speed increases. Negative net work → KE decreases → speed decreases. Zero net work → KE unchanged → constant speed. The work-energy theorem beautifully connects forces (work) to motion (KE) without needing to solve for time.
visualDescription: A before/after KE bar chart with a positive 'net work' bar added, showing KE_final > KE_initial
distractors: The object's direction of motion changes
The object gains PE equal to the work done
The object moves in a circle
Work has no effect on motion

acceptableAlternatives: work done by spring equals decrease in spring PE
quizQuestion: What is the work done by a spring force as the spring goes from compression/extension x_i to x_f?
explanation: W_spring = ½kx_i² − ½kx_f² = −ΔU_s. When a compressed spring (large U) releases to equilibrium (U = 0), it does positive work, converting PE to KE. This is why spring-powered mechanisms store energy. A spring stretched from 0 to x does −½kx² of work on the object doing the stretching (the spring stores this energy as PE).
visualDescription: A compressed spring releasing and accelerating a mass, showing U_s → KE conversion
distractors: W_spring = F × Δx only at equilibrium

correctAnswer: Efficiency = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%
acceptableAlternatives: useful output divided by total input
quizQuestion: How is the efficiency of an energy conversion process defined?
explanation: Efficiency = (useful energy out / total energy in) × 100%. Always ≤ 100% due to second law of thermodynamics — some energy always converts to less useful forms (usually heat). A car engine is typically 20-35% efficient; LED bulbs are ~90% efficient; a pulley system might be 80% efficient. Perfect efficiency (100%) is impossible for heat engines.
visualDescription: A machine with input energy arrow and two output arrows: useful work and wasted heat, with efficiency = useful/total

correctAnswer: Energy bar charts (LOL diagrams) show distribution of K, U, and work
acceptableAlternatives: bar charts showing kinetic, potential, and work-energy accounting
quizQuestion: What tool in AP Physics 1 represents the energy accounting of a system at different moments using bars for K, U_g, U_s, and W?
explanation: Energy bar charts (sometimes called LOL diagrams) visually show how energy is distributed at different moments. Each bar represents an energy type: K (kinetic), U_g (gravitational PE), U_s (elastic PE), and a 'W' bar for work done by external forces. The total height of all bars must remain constant for isolated systems. Used heavily on AP free-response questions.
visualDescription: Two side-by-side bar charts showing energy distribution at two moments, with equal total heights
